items.headline: CBP Confirms CAPE System Launch April 20 with Phase 1 Limitations
items.summary: CBP published CSMS #68315804 confirming that Phase 1 of the CAPE refund system launches April 20, 2026. Phase 1 is limited to unliquidated entries and entries within 80 days of liquidation, covering approximately 63% of IEEPA entries. Refunds expected within 60-90 days of CAPE Declaration acceptance unless compliance concerns arise.
items.source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection / Thompson Hine SmarTrade

items.headline: CIT Judge Endorses CBP's April 20 CAPE Rollout Timeline
items.summary: Judge Eaton ruled that CBP continues to make satisfactory progress toward the April 20 deadline. CBP's four CAPE components are now 60-85% complete. Phase 1 will process refunds within 45 days of acceptance unless compliance issues require further review.
items.source: U.S. Court of International Trade / Thompson Hine SmarTrade

items.headline: CBP Details Final Liquidated Entry Plans in Latest CIT Filing
items.summary: CBP's March 31 declaration to CIT confirms mid-April deployment remains on track and provides first details on handling finally liquidated entries in subsequent CAPE phases. No timeline given for Phase 2 functionality covering entries beyond 90-day voluntary reliquidation period.
items.source: Kelley Drye & Warren / Court of International Trade

items.headline: CIT Orders Refunds for Finally Liquidated IEEPA Entries
items.summary: Court of International Trade amended its order requiring CBP to reliquidate and refund IEEPA tariffs even for entries beyond final liquidation. This expands CBP's obligations beyond Phase 1 capabilities and will require additional CAPE development phases.
items.source: U.S. Court of International Trade / Forvis Mazars

items.headline: Consumer Class Action Wave Targets IEEPA Refund Recipients
items.summary: Over 2,000 consumer class actions filed against companies that passed IEEPA tariff costs to customers, including FedEx, UPS, Costco, and EssilorLuxottica. Plaintiffs claim companies will be unjustly enriched if they retain government refunds without passing savings to consumers who bore the costs.
items.source: Troutman Pepper / Covington & Burling / Arnold & Porter

items.headline: Supreme Court Strikes Down All IEEPA Tariffs as Unlawful
items.summary: Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that IEEPA does not authorize presidential tariff authority, invalidating billions in collected duties. President Trump immediately terminated IEEPA tariffs and replaced them with Section 122 tariffs. Government has until June 8, 2026 to file appeal.
items.source: U.S. Supreme Court / Multiple Legal Publications
